> 
> 7.  Since the genset will not power the entire HVAC

body_html:
12,000 sq ft

--- Greg Hernandez ghernandez@emgroup.net wrote:

Here are a question (2), some assumptions and some
points that should be
considered in the house design:

  1. We are assuming that the genset will provide
    power to the bedrooms,
    kitchen, pool, outside lighting, selected lighting
    throughout the interior
    of the house and communications system.

  2. We need the area (square footage) of the nine
    bedrooms and the kitchen.

  3. The genset for the new house should be less than
    300 ft from the house to
    avoid installing a 13.8kV genset and transfer switch
    in the maintenance
    compound that will have to provide power to the
    complete new house. We are
    looking at different locations for it, i.e. in a
    mechanical room in the
    house or in a self standing enclosure away from the
    house. This location
    needs to be evaluated and considered in the house
    design.

  4. The house design should consider having an
    electrical closet with enough
    space to locate the transfer switch for the genset.

  5. Since the genset will not power the whole house,
    the electrical panels
    and electrical distribution must be coordinated to
    separate the circuits
    that will be supported by emergency power. The
    house's electrical design
    should have one panel for the circuits that will be
    supported by the genset
    and another one for the circuits that do not require
    emergency power.

  6. It might be wise to include one or all wall
    outlets in the rooms that
    will not have emergency power in the genset circuit.

  7. Since the genset will not power the entire HVAC
    system, the HVAC design
    should separate the bedrooms and kitchen from the
    rest of the system.

On 1/17/06 11:15 PM, "J. Epstein"
jeeproject@yahoo.com wrote:

what info do you need....50,000 sq ft... 15 ft
ceilings.. outdoor lights , pool 30x100 nine bed
two
floors

--- Greg Hernandez ghernandez@emgroup.net wrote:

Is it possible for us to get in touch with the
house

designer to get some
data?

On 1/17/06 5:14 PM, "J. Epstein"
jeeproject@yahoo.com wrote:

no /// bedrooms and kitchen///

--- Greg Hernandez ghernandez@emgroup.net
wrote:

I was reviewing the proposed electrical
distribution

with our engineer and I
have some questions.

  1. Would you like emergency generator backup
    for

the

new house?
2. If yes, should the generator be able to run
the

whole house or would you
like to perform some load shedding?
3. Would you like an additional spare backup
generator for redundancy?

In my opinion the emergency generator should be
able

to run the whole house.

Thanks, Greg
